Title: Cysteine and Cysteine-Related Signaling Pathways in Arabidopsis thaliana

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Coordinate manipulation of OAS-A1 and DES1 can tune cytosolic cysteine homeostasis to improve cadmium tolerance and pathogen resistance, but engineering must avoid cysteine overaccumulation and associated toxicity/redox imbalance.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls cytosolic cysteine homeostasis; promotes antioxidant capacity, cadmium tolerance, hypersensitive response competence, and basal pathogen resistance when functional at normal/high activity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA null mutant analysis (oas-a1), overexpression evidence from prior Arabidopsis studies, cysteine quantification, ROS staining, pathogen assays, cadmium tolerance assays, transcriptomic analysis, cell-death observation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cadmium stress; pathogen attack; long-day growth; nutrient-sufficient conditions; oxidative/nitrosative stress via peroxynitrite; developmental stage.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Major cytosolic OASTL isoform; cytosol is the major site of cysteine synthesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Major cytosolic OASTL isoform in Arabidopsis.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: 14-3-3 proteins; SAT isoform(s) in cysteine synthase complex context
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Proteomics (N-terminal acetylation reported by Wirtz et al., 2010); transcriptomics/transcriptomic analysis of oas-a1 mutant (accession not reported).
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OAS-A1 Tyr302 is specifically nitrated by peroxynitrite, reducing O-acetylserine binding and PLP stabilization, thereby inhibiting activity. OAS-A1 is a major determinant of cytosolic redox balance.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced activity causes oxidative stress, dead-cell patches, cadmium sensitivity, reduced pathogen resistance, and impaired hypersensitive response; excessive cysteine may become toxic if overaccumulated.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OAS-A1 is the major contributor to cytosolic cysteine biosynthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. Loss of OAS-A1 reduces intracellular cysteine by about 35%, causes oxidative stress, suppresses senescence markers, and increases cadmium and pathogen sensitivity. 3. OAS-A1 activity is posttranslationally regulated by Tyr nitration, linking cysteine biosynthesis to redox signaling.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reduced sulfur donor for synthesis of essential biomolecules and defense compounds; determinant of antioxidant capacity and redox signaling; supports stress tolerance and immunity.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to manipulate cytosolic sulfide signaling and cysteine turnover for autophagy control and stress adaptation; repression may elevate cysteine but can trigger premature senescence and autophagy, so balanced modulation is required.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Degrades cytosolic cysteine to generate sulfide; maintains cysteine homeostasis and provides sulfide signaling that represses autophagy.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical identification of L-cysteine desulfhydrase activity; T-DNA null mutant analysis (des1); cysteine measurement; ROS assays; senescence markers; autophagy assays including ATG8 accumulation/lipidation; rescue by restoring H2S generation and exogenous sulfide.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Carbon starvation; cadmium stress; pathogen attack; long-day growth; nutrient-sufficient conditions.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Cytosolic enzyme functioning throughout the plant life cycle in cytosolic cysteine turnover.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ATG8 isoforms (functional downstream marker, not direct binding demonstrated)
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript analyses of senescence/pathogen marker genes; no accession reported.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DES1 is described as the only enzyme unequivocally established to degrade cysteine and concomitantly generate H2S in the plant cytosol. Its activity is PLP-dependent.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: des1 loss causes about 25% cysteine increase, premature senescence, constitutive defense-like state, and autophagy activation; altering DES1 may affect growth-defense balance.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DES1 degrades L-cysteine into sulfide, ammonia, and pyruvate, opposing OAS-A1 to maintain cytosolic cysteine homeostasis. 2. des1 mutants accumulate cysteine, show reduced ROS, enhanced cadmium tolerance, premature senescence, and enhanced pathogen resistance. 3. DES1-derived sulfide acts as a cytosolic signal that represses autophagy, as shown by ATG8 lipidation phenotypes and sulfide rescue.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central reduced sulfur donor; precursor of sulfide signaling; regulates antioxidant defenses, immunity, senescence, and autophagy.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A major engineering target to raise cysteine supply and improve cadmium tolerance/accumulation, but should be optimized to avoid cysteine toxicity and redox disturbance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Incorporates sulfide into O-acetylserine to form cysteine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major source of cytosolic cysteine biosynthesis and key determinant of total intracellular cysteine level.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme characterization; T-DNA knockout phenotyping; cysteine quantification; overexpression studies; ROS and stress assays.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cadmium stress; pathogen attack; oxidative/nitrosative stress; developmental stage.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Predominant cytosolic OASTL isoform in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: 14-3-3 proteins; SAT isoform(s) in cysteine synthase complex context
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Proteomics for N-terminal acetylation; transcriptomic analysis of oas-a1 mutant; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specifically inhibited by Tyr302 nitration under peroxynitrite exposure.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced function lowers cysteine and stress tolerance; excessive upregulation may risk cysteine toxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for cytosolic cysteine pool, though not explicitly established as rate-limiting enzyme for whole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OAS-A1 catalyzes the final cysteine-forming step in the cytosol. 2. It is the major contributor to cysteine biosynthesis and its loss lowers cysteine and compromises redox homeostasis. 3. Posttranslational nitration modulates enzyme activity, linking the pathway to stress signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sulfur donor, antioxidant/redox regulator, and precursor for defense-related metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate endogenous sulfide signaling and autophagy; should be manipulated carefully because reduced DES1 causes premature senescence and constitutive defense-related changes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Desulfurates L-cysteine to produce sulfide, ammonia, and pyruvate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls cysteine degradation and sulfide production in the cytosol, thereby influencing cysteine level and signaling outputs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assay; null mutant phenotyping; metabolite measurements; autophagy markers; sulfide rescue assays.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts flux from target product and generates signaling derivative from target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Carbon starvation; cadmium stress; pathogen attack.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript analyses only; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Only unequivocally established cytosolic source of signaling sulfide in Arabidopsis according to the review.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes senescence and autophagy activation despite higher cysteine.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for cytosolic sulfide signaling and cysteine turnover.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DES1 is a bona fide L-cysteine desulfhydrase in the Arabidopsis cytosol. 2. It modulates cysteine homeostasis and generates sulfide used for signaling. 3. DES1-derived sulfide represses autophagy and contributes to stress-related phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/signaling molecule involved in autophagy repression, stress responses, photosynthesis regulation, and stomatal movement.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be leveraged to improve cyanide/sulfide detoxification and root development, but insufficient data for deployment without considering respiration and cyanide homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Incorporates sulfide into O-acetylserine to form cysteine in mitochondria.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes a minor fraction of total OASTL activity but is essential for mitochondrial sulfide/cyanide detoxification and proper root hair elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA null mutant phenotyping; complementation; cyanide measurement; AOX1A expression analysis; root hair assays.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product and detoxifies inhibitory sulfide to sustain cyanide detoxification cycle
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Controlled growth conditions; root tissues; pathogen responses.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed mitochondrial OASTL in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Gene expression analysis of AOX1A; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: An independent study suggested mitochondrial cysteine biosynthesis contributes to sulfide detoxification alongside an OASTL-independent mechanism and is not required for cyanide detoxification; the review emphasizes joint action with CAS-C1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes cyanide accumulation, increased AOX pathway, and defective root hair elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key detoxification-supporting step in mitochondrial cyanide/sulfide homeostasis, not established as globally rate-limiting.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OAS-C is the authentic mitochondrial OASTL producing cysteine from OAS and sulfide. 2. Despite low contribution to total OASTL activity, it is required for mitochondrial detoxification homeostasis and normal root hair elongation. 3. oas-c mutants accumulate cyanide and phenocopy cas-c1 root hair defects, and complementation restores the phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sulfur donor and mitochondrial sulfide detoxification intermediate supporting cyanide detoxification and development.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target to optimize cyanide detoxification, root hair development, and pathogen-response tuning, but manipulation may alter pathogen resistance tradeoffs and respiration.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts cysteine and cyanide to β-cyanoalanine and hydrogen sulfide.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Primary mitochondrial cyanide-detoxifying enzyme; required to maintain low cyanide levels for normal root hair elongation and balanced pathogen responses.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA null mutant characterization; cyanide quantification; AOX1A expression; root hair phenotyping; complementation; exogenous cyanide phenocopy; hydroxocobalamin rescue; pathogen assays.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for detoxification of inhibitory by-product and generates signaling-related cyanide detoxification product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Vertical plate growth; exogenous cyanide; hydroxocobalamin treatment; pathogen infection by Botrytis cinerea, Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000, and Beet curly top virus.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed mitochondrial protein; transcriptionally regulated during plant-pathogen interactions.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed mitochondrial enzyme in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Gene expression analyses during pathogen interactions; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Formerly named CYS-C1. Generates sulfide as coproduct, which can also inhibit cytochrome c oxidase and may need detoxification by OAS-C.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes cyanide accumulation, root hair defects, increased susceptibility to necrotrophs, increased tolerance to biotrophs/virus, and altered respiration via AOX induction.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Major control point for mitochondrial cyanide detoxification.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CAS-C1 is the main mitochondrial enzyme detoxifying cyanide by forming β-cyanoalanine. 2. cas-c1 mutants accumulate cyanide, induce AOX1A, and fail in root hair elongation; these defects are rescued by complementation or cyanide antidote treatment. 3. CAS-C1 also modulates pathogen responses through cyanide-dependent signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intermediate for cyanide detoxification and nitrogen recycling to Asn/Asp/ammonia.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Likely secondary target because knockout causes no obvious phenotype under tested photoperiods; however, balancing OAS-B with SCS may matter in chloroplast OAS competition and photoprotection engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Incorporates sulfide into O-acetylserine to form cysteine in chloroplast stroma.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Chloroplastic OASTL isoform contributing to cysteine synthesis, but not the main determinant of total leaf OASTL activity under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout analysis; enzyme characterization; localization studies.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day and short-day photoperiods; light/stress context.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Located in stroma, unlike lumen-localized SCS. Competes with SCS for OAS substrate.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: High OAS-B activity may limit OAS availability for S-sulfocysteine synthesis in lumen, potentially affecting photoprotection.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OAS-B is the stromal chloroplast OASTL catalyzing cysteine formation from OAS and sulfide. 2. Loss of OAS-B alone causes no apparent phenotype under long- or short-day conditions, indicating it is not the main determinant of leaf OASTL activity. 3. OAS-B competes with SCS for OAS and may influence chloroplast redox/signaling indirectly.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sulfur donor for plastid metabolism and redox-related processes.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for improving photoprotection and tolerance to prolonged light by enhancing S-sulfocysteine-mediated chloroplast redox control; deployment should consider substrate competition with OAS-B and OAS supply.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Incorporates thiosulfate into O-acetylserine to form S-sulfocysteine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces chloroplastic S-sulfocysteine required for protection of photosystems and redox balance, especially under long-day/light-stress conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Null mutant characterization under different photoperiods; genetic complementation; ROS detection; photosynthetic measurements; localization studies.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day versus short-day photoperiod; excess light/high-light-like conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Minor chloroplast isoform active in light-dependent redox control.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Physiological/photosynthetic phenotyping only; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Formerly CS26. Localized in thylakoid lumen rather than stroma. Suggested to sense thiosulfate accumulation and generate S-sulfocysteine as a mild oxidant signal.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes chlorosis, reduced size, ROS accumulation, and impaired photosynthesis under long days; likely vulnerable to prolonged light stress.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for chloroplast S-sulfocysteine-dependent photoprotection.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SCS is a chloroplast lumen S-sulfocysteine synthase that uses OAS and thiosulfate to generate S-sulfocysteine. 2. scs mutants are specifically defective under long-day conditions, showing ROS accumulation, chlorosis, reduced growth, and impaired photosynthesis. 3. S-sulfocysteine is proposed to act as a chloroplast redox signal protecting photosystems under prolonged light exposure.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/signaling metabolite protecting photosystems and chloroplast electron transport under excess light.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory node to improve chloroplast cysteine/OAS partitioning and stress resilience, but effects are indirect and context-dependent; best considered in combination with SAT/OAS-B/SCS engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplastic cysteine synthesis complex formation and is required for full cysteine synthesis and stress tolerance under light/stress conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (cyp20-3), protein interaction evidence with chloroplastic SAT, phenotypic comparison under light/stress conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller that likely modulates OAS synthesis and thereby affects cysteine and possibly S-sulfocysteine production in chloroplasts.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Precursor supply for cysteine synthesis and likely chloroplast redox/signaling balance.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review suggests, but does not directly prove, that compromised S-sulfocysteine synthesis/redox regulation in cyp20-3 may explain its phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: cyp20-3 mutants show severe stress phenotype under light/stress conditions and reduced cysteine synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Chloroplastic SAT isoform; OAS-B-associated cysteine synthase complex
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Yes; may mediate association of SAT and OAS-B into the cysteine synthase complex in chloroplasts.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes functional SAT–OAS-B complex formation rather than direct transcriptional regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP20-3 interacts with chloroplastic SAT and may help assemble SAT with OAS-B in the cysteine synthase complex. 2. cyp20-3 mutants have reduced cysteine synthesis and severe stress phenotypes under light/stress conditions. 3. Its phenotype resembles scs, suggesting a possible indirect role in chloroplast S-sulfocysteine/redox regulation, though this remains untested.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sulfur donor and redox-related metabolite supporting chloroplast stress tolerance.
